does: This paper introduces EvoSafety, a framework for improving LLM safety through externalized attack-defense mechanisms that operate independently of target model architectures. The core contribution separates red-teaming and defense into reusable external structures: attacks leverage a learnable adversarial skill library that can be expanded to overcome saturation and discover novel failure modes, while defenses employ a lightweight auxiliary model with memory retrieval rather than model-specific fine-tuning. The framework enables safety improvements to propagate across different victim models through library and memory updates alone, eliminating the need to retrain or modify the underlying language models, and demonstrates that this approach achieves robust safety improvements that generalize beyond the models used during training.
connects: This work addresses fundamental limitations in existing adversarial red-teaming and safety fine-tuning paradigms for large language models. It builds on prior research coupling red-teaming and post-training in closed loops, recognizing that these approaches suffer from attack saturation and produce rigid, model-specific defenses that fail to transfer. The paper extends research on adversarial skill discovery and compositional attack generation by introducing persistent external skill libraries, and it relates to broader work on auxiliary defense mechanisms and retrieval-augmented safety approaches. The model-agnostic design responds to challenges in scaling safety across diverse model architectures and parameter counts, building on insights that decoupled safety mechanisms can better generalize than tightly integrated approaches.
enables: This framework opens several future research directions in LLM safety. The externalized structure enables continual safety improvement through library expansion without retraining victim models, supporting lifelong safety maintenance as new attack vectors emerge. The model-agnostic defense approach suggests pathways toward universal safety mechanisms applicable across future model families and scales. The inspectable attack-defense co-evolution enables better understanding of vulnerability landscapes and failure modes through evolved attack representations. The memory-based defense mechanism points toward efficient safety updates in deployed systems and supports safer multi-model deployments. This work also enables research into optimal skill library design, emergent properties of adversarial skill combinations, and how external safety structures can enforce formal robustness guarantees across heterogeneous model populations.
